title: A nanoscale magnetic spectrum analyzer based on qubit dressed states
authors: Jan Rueschkamp, Shantam Ravan, Daniel Fernandez, Nazar Delegan, F. Joseph Heremans, David D. Awschalom, Ronald L. Walsworth, Nikola Maksimovic, Amir Yacoby
abstract: Magnetic field fluctuations on nanometer length scales manifest in a diverse range of phenomena -- electron and spin dynamics in materials and devices, quantum many-body systems, and molecular chemistry. Measuring these phenomena requires sensors with a challenging combination of broad spectral bandwidth, high sensitivity, and nanoscale spatial resolution. Nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers, atom-like quantum sensors in diamond, possess the requisite sensitivity and nanoscale sensing volume, but are typically limited in bandwidth by the practical speed of the applied quantum control sequence. Here, we overcome this limitation by exposing the NV qubit to a microwave dressing field during a dynamical decoupling sequence, which both amplifies and frequency-mixes target signals at arbitrary frequencies into the detection band of the dynamical decoupling protocol. We demonstrate this approach by using NV centers to detect both coherent and noisy nanoscale spin wave dynamics in a magnetic yttrium-iron-garnet (YIG) thin film over a broad frequency range. Our technique generalizes to other qubit platforms, providing a versatile framework for nanoscale spectroscopy across diverse physical and chemical systems.
summary: The authors developed a novel method using microwave dressing fields on NV centers to create a broadband magnetic spectrum analyzer. This technique overcomes the natural frequency bandwidth limits of conventional qubit measurements by mixing external noise signals into the measurable detection band. The results demonstrate high sensitivity, allowing characterization of nanoscale spin dynamics in materials like YIG films.
main_problem: The primary challenge is extending the frequency bandwidth of nanoscale magnetic field measurements beyond the limitations imposed by conventional quantum control sequences (like Rabi rates).
main_result: By dressing the NV qubit with a microwave field during dynamical decoupling, the technique successfully converts the qubit into a broadband spectrum analyzer capable of detecting noise across several GHz.
method: The method uses the quadratic dependence of the AC Stark shift on the total magnetic field amplitude to frequency-mix arbitrary external signals into the measurable detection band of the spin echo sequence.
model_or_system: The system utilizes Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) centers in diamond as solid-state quantum sensors, coupled to materials like YIG thin films. The physics is described by analyzing the phase variance ($\langle\phi^2angle$) accumulated by the qubit due to external magnetic noise.
key_observables: Phase variance ($\langle\phi^2angle$), AC Stark shift ($\delta E$), and magnon group velocity ($v_{	ext{magnon}}$).
important_parameters: Detuning ($\Delta$), probe field strength ($\Omega_{	ext{probe}}$), echo time ($	au$), and the noise spectral density $S(f)$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the noise spectrum is much broader than the filter function width, and various approximations are made regarding signal field terms in the Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup (optical/microwave), the dressed state spin echo sequence flow diagram, and plots showing $\langle\phi^2angle$ vs. probe frequency for both broadband noise detection and magnon spectrum fitting.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitations of conventional sensing, develops the theoretical framework based on dressing fields to achieve spectral mixing, demonstrates this capability by measuring broadband noise in YIG films, and validates the technique by extracting material properties like magnon group velocity.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, tunable quantum transduction mechanism that effectively bypasses hardware bandwidth limitations inherent in solid-state qubits, making it highly relevant for advanced quantum sensing applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Measurement of qubit dressed states using a spin echo protocol. (a) Dressed state spin echo sequence with a probe field during one arm of length τ, which acts as an effective B field on the qubit (see supple- mental section B 1). Bloch sphere representation (above) shows effect of pulse sequence on qubit spin. (b) Calcu- lated dressed state energies (within the rotating frame) in comparison to the qubit bare states as a function of detuning, ∆, of the probe field from the bare resonance relative to the probe field Rabi rate, Ωprobe. The black dotted line describes the bare qubit excited state in the rotating frame, while the dashed line gives the summed energy of the bare qubit...

Figure 3. Scaling of dressed state DD RF noise mea- surements on experimental parameters. (a) Dependence of the experimentally determined NV spin phase vari- ance ⟨ϕ2⟩on probe field amplitude, Bprobe, measurement time, τ, and probe field detuning from the NV resonance, ∆. The data is well-fitted by the scalings predicted by our model of this measurement scheme (Eq. 4). (b) Es- timate of noise floor, i.e. minimal detectable RF noise spectral density, as a function of Rabi rate Ωprobe and noise signal frequency f, obtained by extrapolating the noise floor in our experiment for one second of integra- tion where the Rabi rate ≈2.5 MHz.

Figure 2. Measurement of broadband noise spectra us- ing qubit dressed states. (a) Dressed state spin echo sequence with probe field and noisy RF signal during both arms of length τ. The noise mixes with the probe field via the quadratic dependence of the qubit AC Stark shift on the total field amplitude. This mixing effectively imprints the noise onto the probe field, producing a fluc- tuating effective Rabi rate that induces decoherence in the qubit’s x–y plane. Sweeping the spin echo sequence filter function, which is set by the probe field frequency fprobe, and repeating spin echo measurements allows de- termination of S, the noise spectral density of the RF signal field. Bloch sphere...

Fig. 3(b) further compares the noise floor of our dressed state DD to that of conventional DD noise magnetometry under comparable conditions. Con- ventional DD noise magnetometry is analyzed via the following relationship between ϕ2 and the noise floor η. In the limit of target noisy signal frequency much greater than DD sensing frequency, the decoherence signal in DD is given by ϕ2 = γ2Wτ(f) ∗τeff ∗η, where Wτ(f) ∼1/f 2, and the effective evolution time, τeff, is the total evolution time minus the time spent on π-pulses (∼1/ΩRabi). In Fig. 3(b), the Rabi rate in conventional DD is set equal to the probe field strength in dressed state DD for the purpose of comparing the two protocols.

Figure 4. Probing the spin wave properties of YIG using dressed state DD. (a) Experimental setup consists of a gold stripline antenna that produces a MW probe field to coherently drives the electronic spins of an NV ensemble in two ways: directly and by launching magnons into the thin YIG film beneath. The NV spins are also affected by intrinsic thermal magnon MW noise produced by the YIG. (b) Dressed state spin echo sequence, for which the experimentally determined phase variance ⟨ϕ2⟩of the NV sensor as a function of probe field frequency re- solves both the NV bare resonance (|0⟩→| −1⟩) and thermally populated magnons at the FMR enhanced by quadratic mixing with the coherent magnons...

Spintronics.note: The new paper directly measures nanoscale magnetic dynamics in YIG thin films using NV centers, which falls under the general theme of coupling quantum optics (NV center) with magnetic systems (YIG magnons).
